title: Burton and Speke — sources of the Nile

intro.title: Two Lakes and a Quarrel
intro.body: Richard Burton and John Hanning Speke crossed German East Africa in 1857-1859 searching for the source of the Nile. They reached Lake Tanganyika together; Speke alone pressed north and sighted Lake Victoria. The dispute over which lake was the source ended only with Speke's death in 1864.

stops.name: Zanzibar
stops.note: Departure preparations, December 1856 - June 1857. Burton hires Sidi Mubarak Bombay as headman.
stops.imagePrompt: Hand-coloured plate. Sultan's palace overlooking the harbour, two Englishmen in waistcoats arguing in a courtyard, porters loading crates.

stops.name: Bagamoyo
stops.note: Mainland departure 27 June 1857. The party of 132 porters strikes inland for the unknown lakes.
stops.imagePrompt: Hand-coloured plate. Equatorial beach at dawn, surf, long string of porters with chests on heads ascending into bush.

stops.name: Tabora
stops.note: Arab depot. Burton and Speke recover here from malaria for five months. Burton studies Swahili manuscripts.
stops.imagePrompt: Hand-coloured plate. Arab-style flat-roofed houses behind a thorn fence, English officers sick on string beds under a verandah.

stops.name: Lake Tanganyika (Ujiji)
stops.note: 14 February 1858. First Europeans on Tanganyika. Burton too ill to walk; Speke partly blind. They paddle north in a dugout looking for an outlet.
stops.imagePrompt: Hand-coloured plate. Vast inland sea seen from a wooded hill, native dugout on the beach, two haggard Europeans in slings on porters' backs.

stops.name: Lake Victoria (Speke Gulf)
stops.note: 30 July 1858. Speke alone reaches and names the great lake, claims it as the Nile source. Burton stays at Tabora and disputes the identification for the rest of his life.
stops.imagePrompt: Hand-coloured plate. Sunlit inland sea with low islands, single European in pith helmet on a granite rock, porters in foreground.
